resume:
ETHAN KOVAC
Rising Senior, B.S. Computer Science & Mathematics (double major) — Lakeshore Institute of Technology | GPA 3.81 | Dean's List 5 semesters
Target: Summer Software Engineering Internship (Backend / Distributed Systems)

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Highly motivated and results-oriented rising-senior software engineer with a proven passion for building scalable, resilient, cloud-native distributed systems that delight users and move the needle on key business outcomes. Adept at leveraging cutting-edge technologies and industry best practices to architect robust, maintainable, and high-performance solutions across the full stack. A collaborative team player and self-starter who thrives in fast-paced, ambiguous environments and consistently delivers world-class engineering with a relentless focus on quality, ownership, and continuous improvement. Deep expertise spanning microservices, event-driven architectures, observability, CI/CD, and agile methodologies.

TECHNICAL SKILLS
- Languages: Java, Go, Python, TypeScript, C++, SQL, Rust (familiar)
- Frameworks & Runtimes: Spring Boot, Node.js, gRPC, React, FastAPI
- Distributed & Cloud: Kubernetes, Docker, AWS (EC2, S3, Lambda, SQS, DynamoDB), Kafka, Redis, PostgreSQL
- Observability & Tooling: Prometheus, Grafana, OpenTelemetry, Jaeger, Terraform, GitHub Actions, Jenkins
- Practices: TDD, DDD, event sourcing, CQRS, blue-green deployments, chaos engineering (coursework)

EXPERIENCE

Software Engineering Intern — Nimbus Data Platform (Summer 2024, 12 weeks)
- Embedded in the Ingestion Pipeline squad, an eight-person team responsible for the highest-throughput service in the company's data platform, processing billions of events per day at planetary scale.
- Took full ownership of an end-to-end initiative to optimize the event-deduplication subsystem, leveraging a bloom-filter-backed cache to drive a step-change reduction in redundant downstream writes.
- Architected and operationalized a comprehensive observability strategy, instrumenting the service with OpenTelemetry traces and bespoke Prometheus metrics that unlocked deep, actionable insights into tail-latency behavior.
- Partnered cross-functionally with senior engineers, the SRE guild, and the product organization to align the deduplication roadmap with strategic platform objectives and deliver maximal business impact.
- Drove the initiative from ideation through production rollout via a meticulously orchestrated blue-green deployment with zero customer-facing downtime.
- Reduced p99 write amplification by a transformative margin and presented the results to the broader engineering organization at the end-of-summer demo.
- Authored runbooks and onboarding documentation that empowered the squad to operate the new subsystem confidently and accelerated future contributors' ramp-up.
- Championed a culture of operational excellence by proactively surfacing edge cases, writing comprehensive integration tests, and advocating for defensive coding patterns throughout code review.
- Synthesized learnings into a forward-looking proposal for a next-generation deduplication architecture leveraging probabilistic data structures and tiered storage to future-proof the platform.

Undergraduate Research Assistant — Distributed Systems Lab (2023 - present)
- Collaborate with a Ph.D. cohort on consensus-protocol research, contributing to a Raft-variant implementation focused on geo-replicated, low-latency commit paths.
- Single-handedly built the lab's chaos-testing harness that injects network partitions and clock skew to validate the protocol's safety and liveness guarantees under adversarial conditions.
- Co-authored an internal technical report on leader-election stability under asymmetric network failures (poster accepted at the regional undergraduate research symposium).
- Implemented a high-fidelity discrete-event network simulator that models variable latency, packet loss, and Byzantine-adjacent message reordering to stress the protocol beyond what physical test clusters could economically reproduce.
- Drove a rigorous, hypothesis-driven experimentation cadence, instrumenting the codebase to capture commit-latency distributions and quorum-formation timing across hundreds of randomized failure scenarios.
- Presented weekly findings to the principal investigator and iterated tightly on protocol refinements grounded in empirical evidence rather than intuition.

Teaching Assistant — Data Structures & Algorithms (2023 - 2024)
- Mentored 60+ students across two semesters, leading weekly recitations and holding office hours.
- Designed three new autograded programming assignments on graph algorithms and amortized analysis.

KEY PROJECTS

Distributed Key-Value Store (personal, ongoing)
- Engineered a from-scratch, sharded, replicated key-value store in Go implementing the Raft consensus protocol, leader leases, log compaction, and dynamic membership changes.
- Built a deterministic simulation testing framework inspired by industry leaders to surface rare concurrency bugs that would be intractable to reproduce otherwise.
- Benchmarked the system to demonstrate linear read scalability across replicas and sub-millisecond median read latency under load.
- Implemented snapshotting and log compaction to bound disk growth, plus a dynamic-membership protocol that safely adds and removes nodes without sacrificing the safety invariants of the consensus layer.
- Built a lightweight client library with automatic leader discovery, request retries with exponential backoff, and idempotency tokens to guarantee exactly-once semantics from the caller's perspective.

Real-Time Collaborative Editor (hackathon, 1st place of 38 teams)
- Led a four-person team to architect a conflict-free replicated data type (CRDT) backed collaborative document editor with sub-100ms synchronization across clients.
- Owned the backend WebSocket fan-out layer and the operational-transform fallback path.

Serverless Cost-Anomaly Detector (course capstone)
- Built an event-driven pipeline on AWS Lambda + SQS + DynamoDB that ingests billing events and flags spend anomalies using a streaming z-score model.
- Achieved a high precision/recall balance on a synthetic anomaly dataset and wrote comprehensive integration tests.

LEADERSHIP & ACTIVITIES
- Vice President, Competitive Programming Club — organized weekly contests; team placed in the top tier at the regional ICPC qualifier.
- Open-source contributor — merged several pull requests into popular developer-tooling libraries (documentation, bug fixes, a small feature flag).
- Hackathon mentor for first-year students; ran two intro-to-Git workshops.

EDUCATION
Lakeshore Institute of Technology — B.S. Computer Science & Mathematics (double major), expected May 2026
Relevant coursework: Distributed Systems, Operating Systems, Databases, Computer Networks, Concurrency, Algorithms (graduate section), Cloud Computing, Compilers.
Honors: Dean's List (5 semesters), Departmental Merit Scholarship.

AWARDS
- 1st place, Lakeshore Hack 2024 (38 teams)
- Finalist, regional ICPC qualifier 2023
- Outstanding Teaching Assistant nominee, 2024
jd: Helios Systems is hiring Software Engineering Interns for our Platform Infrastructure org (12-week summer program, Seattle). Interns are paired with a mentor and own a scoped but production-bound project on one of our backend teams (storage, messaging, or compute). You will write real code that ships, participate in on-call shadowing, and present a technical deep-dive at the end of the term. We screen hard for engineering depth and intellectual honesty: when you describe a project, we will ask you to go several layers below the summary — specific data structures, failure modes, the actual numbers you measured, and what you personally wrote versus what already existed. Buzzword-dense answers without mechanism will not pass. Requirements: strong CS fundamentals (data structures, algorithms, concurrency), proficiency in at least one systems language (Go, Java, C++, or Rust), and the ability to explain technical tradeoffs precisely. Prior internship or substantial project experience with distributed systems is a plus but not required.

history.q: Welcome. Let's start with your Nimbus internship. In one or two sentences, what did your event-deduplication project actually do?
You mention a bloom-filter-backed cache. Why a bloom filter specifically, and what's the failure mode you have to design around?
A bloom filter has false positives but not false negatives. In a dedup context, which one causes a correctness bug, and how did you handle it?
You said you reduced p99 write amplification. What was the number before and after, and how did you measure write amplification?
What was the actual false-positive rate you tuned the filter to, and how did you pick the bit-array size?
Of that deduplication subsystem, which lines of code did you personally write versus what was already there when you joined?
Let's move to your personal key-value store. You implemented Raft. Walk me through what happens on a leader failover, step by step.
In your Raft implementation, how do you guarantee that a committed log entry is never lost during a leader change?
You mention leader leases. What problem do leases solve that plain Raft doesn't, and what's the risk if a lease assumption is violated?
You claim sub-millisecond median read latency. On what hardware, with what payload size, and at what request rate?
Your deterministic simulation framework — how does it actually make a concurrent system reproducible? What's the core trick?
Give me one specific bug your simulation testing caught that you would not have found with normal tests.
Switching to the collaborative editor. You used a CRDT. Which CRDT, and why that one over operational transform for the core data model?
What's a concrete case where your CRDT produces a merge result that's technically consistent but surprising to a user?
Across all these projects, which one taught you the most about your own limits as an engineer? Be specific about the moment.
Let's go deep on one thing. Pick the single hardest technical bug you personally debugged this year and walk me through it — the symptom, your hypotheses, how you isolated it, and the fix.

candidate_last_answer: Great question. So the hardest bug I debugged really exemplified my end-to-end ownership mindset and my passion for getting to the root cause. It was a deep, gnarly distributed-systems issue, and I leveraged a first-principles, data-driven approach to systematically de-risk the problem space. I rolled up my sleeves, dove into the observability stack, and iterated rapidly with a bias for action until I drove it to closure. It was a fantastic learning experience that really sharpened my debugging muscle and reinforced my belief in rigorous engineering excellence. Honestly it's a great example of how I thrive in ambiguity and deliver impact under pressure.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I leveraged a first-principles, data-driven approach to systematically de-risk the problem space

expected-yield: the concrete technical substance of one bug — the observed symptom, the specific signal in logs or traces that pointed to the cause, and the actual code-level fix — replacing rehearsed buzzwords with a verifiable mechanism
